paper_title: Autophoresis of a Janus particle near a planar wall: a lubrication limit

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We study the self-diffusiophoresis of a spherical chemically active particle near a planar, impermeable wall, with a focus on the influence of particle orientation on propulsion. We analyze a Janus particle with asymmetric surface chemical activity, consisting of a small inert region within a catalytically active cap. While numerical simulations have been used to study such particles, they encounter difficulties resolving the flow and transport in the extreme near-wall regime due to geometric confinement and steep solute concentration gradients. We address this limitation through an asymptotic analysis in the near-contact limit, where the gap between the particle and the wall is narrow. In particular, we consider the distinguished limit in which the inert region is asymptotically comparable in size to the lubrication region. We analyze an axisymmetric configuration in which the inert face is oriented parallel to the wall and extend the analysis to slightly tilted orientations. We find that the capsize determines whether a tilted particle rotates back toward the axisymmetric state or continues to reorient, thereby characterizing its rotational stability in the near-contact regime.

sections.content: Autophoresis of Janus particle near planar wall: lubrication limit Tachin Ruangkriengsin,1, Gunther Turk,2 and Howard A. Stone3, 1Program in Applied and Computational Mathematics, Princeton University, New Jersey 08544, USA 2Princeton Materials Institute, Princeton University, New Jersey 08544, USA 3Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Princeton University, New Jersey 08544, USA We study the self-diffusiophoresis of spherical chemically active particle near planar, impermeable wall, with focus on the influence of particle orientation on propulsion. We analyze Janus particle with asymmetric surface chemical activity, consisting of small inert region within catalytically active cap. While numerical simulations have been used to study such particles, they encounter difficulties resolving the flow and transport in the extreme near-wall regime due to geometric confinement and steep solute concentration gradients. We address this limitation through an asymptotic analysis in the near-contact limit, where the gap between the particle and the wall is narrow. In particular, we consider the distinguished limit in which the inert region is asymptotically comparable in size to the lubrication region. We analyze an axisymmetric configuration in which the inert face is oriented parallel to the wall and extend the analysis to slightly tilted orientations. We find that the capsize determines whether tilted particle rotates back toward the axisymmetric state or continues to reorient, thereby characterizing its rotational stability in the near-contact regime. I. INTRODUCTION Phoretic motion refers to the transport of colloidal particles driven by gradients in physicochemical fields, such as electric potential, solute concentration, or temperature [1]. These fields interact with the particle within narrow interfacial layer, generating an effective slip velocity at the particle surface. Neglecting inertial effects, the force-free and torque-free conditions on freely suspended particle then determine its rigid-body motion. When the gradients driving phoretic motion are generated by surface reactions at the particle boundary, the resulting mechanism is known as autophoresis, or self-diffusiophoresis, when the relevant field is the chemical concentration. This mechanism was first proposed theoretically by Golestanian et al. [2], who demonstrated that asymmetric production or consumption of solute at the particle surface can lead to self propulsion. Within this framework, the active portions of the surface are modeled by prescribing fixed solute flux density, while the inert portions satisfy the familiar no-flux condition. More recent studies have extended this classical model to incorporate the effects of solute advection and reaction on phoretic propulsion [36]. In the present work, however, we return to the simplest model introduced by Golestanian et al. [2] and focus on the interplay between the anisotropic surface chemistry and confinement. In an unbounded fluid, sustained autophoretic motion requires an asymmetric distribution of surface activity. This requirement has motivated extensive efforts to design and fabricate artificial swimmers with deliberately patterned surface chemistry [7]. An experimental realization of such surface heterogeneity is provided by half-coated colloids (Janus particles), in which one hemisphere is covered with catalytic materials such as platinum. This active surface interacts with the surrounding solution to generate hydrogen peroxide, which serve as the fuel driving the phoretic motion. Experimental studies have demonstrated that these particles exhibit persistent motion and enhanced diffusivity, in agreement with proposed diffusiophoretic mechanisms [812]. In many practical settings, Janus particles operate in confined environments, where nearby boundaries modify the translational and rotational symmetries of their motion. This sensitivity to confinement can be exploited to steer and direct Janus particles through wall-induced hydrodynamic and diffusive interactions. In particular, chemically patterned or topographically structured boundaries have been shown to guide particle trajectories and enable controlled transport and sorting [1318]. Near-wall dynamics can be highly nontrivial, with particles exhibiting behaviors such as skating, hovering, or reflection from the boundary, which can complicate the robust design of both particle properties and boundary geometries [1921]. variety of theoretical approaches have been developed to analyze the behavior of Janus particles near solid boundaries. These include boundary-element methods [18, 19, 2224], multipole expansion techniques [20, 25], eigenfunction expansions in bispherical coordinates [21], and Galerkin-based formulations [26]. Due to the presence of steep solute concentration gradients, these approaches face challenges in resolving Janus particle dynamics in the extreme near-wall regime, where the particlewall separation is small compared to the particle size. For example, Uspal et al. [19] restrict their analysis to particlewall separations exceeding 1.1 particle radii, as boundary-element methods become computationally expensive at smaller gaps. Similarly, Ibrahim and Liverpool [20] and Turk et al. [26] introduce short-range repulsive potentials to prevent particlewall contact when employing tachin@princeton.edu hastone@princeton.edu 6 2 0 F 8 2 ] s . - c [ 1 1 9 7 0 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r 2 multipole expansion and Galerkin-based formulations, respectively, remedy which may degrade the method accuracy in the near-contact regime. Eigenfunction expansions in bispherical coordinates [21] provide one of the few frameworks capable of resolving particlewall interactions down to vanishing separations without artificial regularization. This technique, however, is restricted to simple geometries. In any event, the reliance of the semi-analytical methods on infinite series provides rather physical intuition. In this work, we adopt an asymptotic approach to study the dynamics of Janus particle near wall in regimes that are difficult to access numerically. Of particular relevance are the asymptotic analyses of Yariv [27, 28], who examined the autophoretic motion of an isotropically active particle in the vicinity of solid wall. While Yariv [27, 28] analyzed both remote and near-wall configurations for isotropically active particles, we restrict attention here to the near-wall regime for Janus particle, setting that has been studied in less detail. In this regime, the leading-order particle motion is governed by solute and flow transport within the narrow lubrication gap. Yariv [28] showed that the near-wall behavior of an isotropically active particle coincides with bispherical-coordinate solutions for Janus particle [21] when the lubrication region lies entirely within the active cap. Here, we extend this analysis to Janus geometry with an inert patch of size comparable to the lateral extent of the gap, thereby capturing the combined influence of the active cap and inert face on autophoretic motion near wall. The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows. In Sec. II, we formulate the problem and provide the scalings of the variables and the governing equations. In Sec. III, we analyze an axisymmetric configuration in which the inert face of the particle is oriented parallel to the wall. We resolve the solute concentration and flow fields within the narrow gap and obtain explicit expressions for the hydrodynamic force and the phoretic velocity normal to the wall. In Sec. IV, we extend the analysis to situations in which the inert face is slightly tilted, leading to fully three-dimensional solute concentration and flow fields. We quantify the effect of the tilt on the phoretic velocity parallel to the wall and on the rotational velocity about the tilt axis. We conclude with discussion of the results in Sec. V. II. Although the translational velocity in the 𝑥-direction remains positive for all values of Φ, the angular velocity changes sign at Φ 4.60. This behavior is striking, as it implies change in the rotational stability of the particle. For Janus particle with fixed cap size 𝜙, when the particle is sufficiently far from the wall such that 𝜙/𝜖 1/2 < 4.60, small tilt in its orientation produces restoring rotation that drives the particle back toward the axisymmetric configuration. In contrast, when the particle is extremely close to the wall so that 𝜙/𝜖 1/2 > 4.60, small tilt induces destabilizing rotation, causing the particle to rotate away from the axisymmetric state. This phoretic motion of the Janus particle is illustrated in Fig. 5. It is important to note that the directions of motion predicted here are in qualitative agreement with the bi-spherical coordinate analysis of Mozaffari et al. [21], although detailed comparison is limited because our study focuses on the slightly tilted regime. Mozaffari et al. [21] observed that swimmers with larger active areas tend to rotate clockwise as they approach wall, whereas at very small separations the torque reverses and counterclockwise rotation dominates (see discussions on page 20 or Fig. 10 of Mozaffari et al. [21]). These remarks are consistent with our analysis. However, Mozaffari et al. [21] did not comment on how these changes in the direction of motion arise in the lubrication regime, nor did they discuss how the directions depend on the parameter Φ = 𝜙/𝜖 1/2, which relates the cap size to the gap distance. FIG. 5: Schematic illustration of slightly tilted Janus particle of radius 𝑎 near planar wall, indicating the direction of its motion. For all values of Φ = 𝜙/𝜖 1/2, the particle translates in the positive 𝑥and 𝑧-directions. In contrast, the direction of rotation depends on Φ: (left) for Φ 4.6, the particle rotates counterclockwise, whereas (right) for Φ 4.6, it rotates clockwise. Similar to the analysis in Sec. III C, our framework in this section also applies to the complementary configuration consisting of small active cap and large inert face, with the active cap oriented toward the wall. We find that the direction of motion in this complementary configuration is nearly opposite to that of the original one. Specifically, when the particle is close (but not extremely close) to the wall, it rotates counterclockwise, whereas in the extreme near-wall limit it rotates clockwise. The translational motion parallel to the wall, 𝑉𝑥, is in the negative-𝑥 direction, while the vertical velocity 𝑉𝑧 has the same direction as in the original configuration, albeit with different magnitude, as noted in Sec. III C. V. CONCLUSION In this study, we examined the dynamics of Janus particle near solid wall. For an axisymmetric configuration in which the inert face is oriented parallel to the boundary, we obtained the vertical hydrodynamic force acting on the particle for both the original and complementary configurations, as illustrated in Fig. 2. In the near-contact regime, this hydrodynamic force is dominated by the lubrication force within the narrow particlewall gap and is determined by the extent to which the wall effectively sees the active portion of the particle; even small inert face or, conversely, small active cap can have pronounced influence on the particles motion when it is brought sufficiently close to the wall. This behavior is captured by the dimensionless parameter Φ = 𝜙/𝜖 1/2, which compares the cap size to the square root of the gap distance. Although the analysis of the axisymmetric configuration in Sec. III is relatively straightforward, the methods developed to treat mixed boundary conditions in the lubrication regime, detailed in Appendix A, are broadly applicable and readily extensible to more complex boundary conditions, such as those arising from periodically patterned Janus particles or, more generally, lubrication flows with mixed boundary conditions. We are not aware of prior work that establishes the continuity of the solute concentration and its first derivative through matched-asymptotic arguments; the present framework therefore provides systematic asymptotic basis for identifying regularity of key physical quantities in related problems. When the particle is slightly tilted, with tilt angle 𝜓 1, the solute concentration and flow fields become fully threedimensional. We therefore employ double perturbation expansion in 𝜖 and Ψ = 𝜓/𝜖 1/2 to determine the leading-order effects of the tilt on the particles motion. While the translational velocity parallel to the wall remains positive for all values of Φ, the rotational velocity about the tilt axis changes sign at finite value of Φ. This direction of motion is consistent with the findings of Mozaffari et al. [21] and demonstrates that the axisymmetric configuration may be either rotationally stable or unstable, depending depending on the particlewall separation in the near-contact regime One limitation of the present work is the assumption of slight tilt angle, Ψ 1. Previous studies have reported that Janus particles may skate along wall at finite, steady tilt angle [1921, 26]. Our analysis cannot capture this behavior, as the tilt introduces only an 𝑂 (Ψ) correction to the 𝑂 (1) velocity in the normal direction to the wall given by (34). full solution of (41) subject to the boundary conditions (42) in the regime Ψ = 𝑂 (1) would determine whether steady skating states occur in the lubrication limit. Although the calculations are not presented here, we have analyzed the two-dimensional counterpart of the Janus particlewall problem and found that steady skating does not occur for any finite tilt angle Ψ = 𝑂 (1) in the lubrication limit. This suggests that such skating behavior may be intrinsic to the three-dimensional setting. Another promising avenue for future research is to extend the present analysis to incorporate solute reaction and advection, in the spirit of Michelin and Lauga [6]. In particular, Yariv [28] demonstrated that, in the presence of surface reactions, the propulsion velocity of an isotropic active particle exhibits an anomalous scaling with 𝜖 that depends on the Damkohler number, which quantifies the relative importance of reaction and diffusion. Incorporating these effects into the present framework could reveal nontrivial behaviors of Janus particles even in the lubrication limit and improve quantitative agreement with numerical and experimental studies. 14 We thank Ehud Yariv for helpful discussions.
